events.intro: Lifecycle events fire after every mutation. Callbacks are async-safe. Errors inside a callback are caught and logged without interrupting the HTTP response or service call.

types.fires: After a new document is inserted.
After any field on a document changes.
After a specific named field changes. One event per changed key.
When any element in an array field changes.
When the array element whose keyField === keyValue changes.
After a document is soft or hard deleted.
After a soft-deleted document is restored.

events.nullability: TypeScript resolves callback argument nullability from the event string at compile time. update and update.* callbacks receive non-null Partial<T> for both whatWas and whatIs. create and restore callbacks receive null for both. delete callbacks receive non-null whatWas and null for whatIs.

examples.label: Send email on status change
Watch a specific token in extra_data
Rebuild search index on create or update
Audit log on delete

interceptors.intro: Interceptors are standard Express (req, res, next) middleware functions mounted per action type. They run before the controller handler and can block, modify, or pass through the request. In addition to broad action types, update interceptors can be narrowed to specific field paths so they only fire when a request targets that field, not on every update.
notes: Multiple interceptors for the same action run in registration order.
The 'all' action type matches every request regardless of method.
Each interceptor must call next() to continue or send a response to halt.
Broad 'update' fires on any update regardless of which fields are in the body.
'update.fieldName' fires only when the request targets that field - checked against req.body keys, req.params.field (for PATCH /:id/field/:field), and req.body.field (for nested ops).
A parent path pattern 'update.personal_information' also fires for deeper child paths like 'personal_information.email'.
Multiple field patterns can be passed as an array and the interceptor fires if any one of them matches.

examples.label: RBAC on delete (broad)
Guard a specific field - update.provider_id
Guard a nested object path - update.personal_information
Multiple field patterns in one interceptor
Scope reads to the current user (broad read)
Inject updated_by on every write (broad all-update)

fieldResolution.title: How fields are resolved per route

nested.intro: Nested operations target nested objects and arrays using MongoDB operators directly, bypassing the shallow Object.assign merge used by the standard update method. Every object inside an array receives an auto-injected _mmid (a stable UUID) that gives each item an addressable identity without requiring a sub-schema.
nested.mmid: _mmid is injected automatically on save and on document init. It persists across updates. Use it as the stable identity key for patch_item and pull_id instead of array index, which shifts when items are added or removed.

operations.name: push_many
operations.value: An array of items to append.

operations.name: pull
operations.value: A filter object. Items matching the filter are removed.

operations.name: pull_id
operations.value: The _mmid string of the item to remove.

operations.name: add_to_set
operations.value: A single value. Appended only if not already present.

operations.name: increment
operations.value: A number. Use negative values to decrement.

relations.intro: Entities declare relations to other entities at initialisation time. Relations enforce foreign keys at the schema level and enable automatic population at query time.

types.name: parent
types.summary: Declares that this entity belongs to another. A required foreign key field is injected into the schema.

types.name: children
types.summary: Declares which entities are children of this one. Child results are populated on withChildren calls as paginated sub-collections.

types.name: sister
types.summary: Declares a peer relation. A required foreign key is injected. Sister entities do not nest inside each other but their FK constraint ensures data consistency.

validation.intro: Validation uses Joi schemas defined at entity initialisation. Reserved fields (uuid, slug, created_at, updated_at, deleted_at) are stripped automatically and never validated from user input.
validation.createSchema: Joi schema map for fields accepted on create. Required constraints are enforced.
validation.updateSchema: Joi schema map for fields accepted on update. If omitted, defaults to createSchema with all keys set to optional automatically.
validation.options: createValidationOptions and updateValidationOptions accept any Joi.ValidationOptions. The default is { abortEarly: false, allowUnknown: true }.
validation.skipValidation: Pass skipValidation: true to CreateOptions or UpdateOptions to bypass Joi entirely for that call. Useful for seeding or internal migrations.
validation.programmatic: Call entity.service.validate(data, 'create') or entity.service.validate(data, 'update') to run validation without inserting. Returns { valid: boolean, errors: string[] }.

typescript.intro: The package ships complete .d.ts declarations. All generics are optional - the package works in plain JavaScript without any type annotations.
typescript.extending: Extend BaseEntityDocument to type your additional fields. Pass the interface as the generic to MetaEntity.
typescript.eventPaths: EventPath<T> produces a union of all valid event strings including typed dot-paths under update.*. TypeScript's IDE autocomplete surfaces these as suggestions.
typescript.callbackTypes: CallbackForEvent<T, E> resolves the correct callback signature for a given event string E. update.* callbacks receive non-null Partial<T> for both whatWas and whatIs. create and restore receive null. delete receives non-null whatWas and null whatIs.

appendData.intro: res.appendData() lets interceptors inject extra data that gets merged into the response data object before it is sent. It is injected onto every response automatically by the interceptor pipeline.
appendData.usage: Call res.appendData({ key: value }) inside any interceptor. Multiple calls are merged together.
appendData.mergeRule: If response.data is an object, the appendData payload is spread directly into it. If response.data is an array or primitive, it is wrapped: { data: originalData, ...appendedPayload }.

append.intro: The append query parameter and service option hydrates related documents from any MongoDB collection onto a response, without a schema-level relation. Works with MetaEntity collections and plain Mongoose models alike.
append.syntax: Format per directive: collectionName-localField. collectionName is matched case-insensitively against mongoose.models. localField is the field on the source document whose value is the ID to look up. If no hyphen is present, localField defaults to collectionName + Id.
append.resultKey: The related document is placed under a key equal to the lowercased collection name.
append.resolution: For single-document responses a single findOne is issued per directive. For list responses a single $in batch query is issued per directive, eliminating N+1.
append.nonMetaCollections: Any Mongoose model compiled in your application is addressable because the resolver reads from mongoose.models directly. The target collection does not need to be a MetaEntity.

analysis.intro: Entity analysis runs aggregation queries against a collection and returns typed result objects. All analysis types respect an optional filter, a time window, and a date field. Results can trigger lifecycle events when a metric crosses a threshold.

types.label: count
types.summary: Total document count matching the filter in the given time window.
types.example: await entity.analyze({ type: "count", window: { from: "2026-05-01", to: "2026-05-28" } })

types.label: growth
types.summary: Percentage and absolute change in document count between the current window and the equal-length period immediately before it.
types.result: { type, current, previous, growthPercent, growthAbsolute, currentWindow, previousWindow }
types.example:
await entity.analyze({ type: "growth", window: { from: yesterday, to: now } })
// growthPercent: 33.33 means 33% more documents than the prior equal period

types.label: sum
types.summary: Sum of a numeric field across all matching documents in the window.
types.example:
await entity.analyze({ type: "sum", field: "amount",
  filter: { category: "income" }, window: { from, to } })

types.label: average
types.summary: Arithmetic mean of a numeric field.
types.example: await entity.analyze({ type: "average", field: "rating", window: { from, to } })

types.label: min / max
types.summary: Minimum, maximum, and range of a numeric field.
types.result: { type, field, min, max, range, count, window }
types.example: await entity.analyze({ type: "min_max", field: "amount", filter: { category: "expense" } })

types.label: distribution
types.summary: Count and percentage per distinct value of a field. Equivalent to GROUP BY with ORDER BY count DESC.
types.result: { type, field, buckets: [{ value, count, percent }], total, window }
types.example:
await entity.analyze({ type: "distribution", groupBy: "status",
  filter: { owned_by: userId }, window: { from, to } })

types.label: timeseries
types.summary: Document count or numeric field sum bucketed into equal time intervals over the window.
types.result: { type, interval, metric, field?, points: [{ bucket, value }], window }

types.label: top
types.summary: Top N documents ranked by a numeric field in descending order.
types.result: { type, field, limit, items: Document[], window }

types.label: rate
types.summary: Documents created per time unit over the window. E.g. average orders per day.
types.result: { type, interval, rate, total, intervals, window }

types.label: field change
types.summary: Absolute and percentage change in a numeric field's sum between the current window and the prior equal-length period.
types.result: { type, field, current, previous, deltaAbsolute, deltaPercent, currentWindow, previousWindow }

types.label: funnel
types.summary: Conversion counts and drop-off percentages across an ordered sequence of field values. Useful for tracking status progressions.
types.result: { type, field, stages: [{ value, count, percent, dropoffPercent }], window }

types.label: percentile
types.summary: p50, p75, p90, p95, and p99 distribution of a numeric field.
types.result: { type, field, p50, p75, p90, p95, p99, count, window }

commonOptions.note: Time window. from and to accept ISO strings or Date objects. Defaults to last 24h.
Document field used for window filtering. Defaults to created_at.
Arbitrary filter applied to every query inside the analysis. Same as the filter option on service.all().
The numeric field to aggregate. Required for sum, average, min_max, top, field_change, percentile, and timeseries with metric=sum.
Field to group distinct values by. Required for distribution and funnel.
Time bucket size for timeseries and rate. Defaults to day.
Max buckets for distribution, or top-N for top. Defaults to 10 or 20 depending on type.
Ordered funnel stage values. Required for funnel. Min 2 values.
Fires analysis.{type}_threshold event when the named result metric crosses the given value.

thresholds.intro: Pass a threshold config to any analysis call. When the specified result metric crosses the threshold the analysis.{type}_threshold event fires on the entity's event emitter.

fields.note: Key on the result object to compare. e.g. count, total, growthPercent, rate, deltaPercent.
gt, gte, lt, lte, or eq.

http.intro: Every entity automatically exposes GET and POST endpoints at /analysis/:type. Query params map directly to analysis options.
http.getExample:
# Count with filter and window
GET /jobs/analysis/count?from=2026-05-01&to=2026-05-28&filter[status]=completed

# Growth for a specific provider
GET /jobs/analysis/growth?from=2026-05-14&to=2026-05-28&filter[provider_id]=uuid-here

# Daily revenue timeseries for income category
GET /transactions/analysis/timeseries?metric=sum&field=amount&interval=day&filter[category]=income&from=2026-05-01&to=2026-05-28

# Funnel via GET with comma-separated stages
GET /jobs/analysis/funnel?groupBy=job_status&stages=pending,accepted,in_progress,completed&from=2026-05-01&to=2026-05-28
http.postExample:
# POST supports larger filter/stages payloads in the body
POST /jobs/analysis/funnel
{
  "groupBy": "job_status",
  "stages": ["pending", "accepted", "in_progress", "completed"],
  "from": "2026-05-01",
  "to": "2026-05-28",
  "filter": { "provider_id": "uuid-here", "category": "income" },
  "threshold_metric": "count",
  "threshold_operator": "lt",
  "threshold_value": 10
}
